title: 2022 AIME II Problems/Problem 4
problem: There is a positive real number $x$ not equal to either $\tfrac{1}{20}$ or $\tfrac{1}{2}$ such that \[\log_{20x} (22x)=\log_{2x} (202x).\] The value $\log_{20x} (22x)$ can be written as $\log_{10} (\tfrac{m}{n})$ , where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Find $m+n$ .
solution: Define $a$ to be $\log_{20x} (22x) = \log_{2x} (202x)$ , what we are looking for. Then, by the definition of the logarithm, \[\begin{cases} (20x)^{a} &= 22x \\ (2x)^{a} &= 202x. \end{cases}\] Dividing the first equation by the second equation gives us $10^a = \frac{11}{101}$ , so by the definition of logs, $a = \log_{10} \frac{11}{101}$ . This is what the problem asked for, so the fraction $\frac{11}{101}$ gives us $m+n = \boxed{112}$ . ~ihatemath123
